answer:
**In Cynewulf's *Andreas* (chapter 1), Andrew realizes that Christ—disguised as the helmsman—had been steering the ship after they reach Mermedonia and Christ reveals himself in the form of a youth.[^1]**

**At that moment, Andrew falls to the ground and addresses Christ directly, confessing his failure to recognize Him during the voyage despite praising the helmsman's extraordinary skill at the stern:**  
“How did I do this, Wielder of men, sinful against myself, Savior of souls, that I, you so good, could not perceive on the path of the waves, where I spoke more of my words before my Maker than I should have?”[^2]

**Earlier on the ship, not yet realizing the helmsman's identity, Andrew had marveled at Christ's steering prowess (noting the vessel's unnatural steadiness, speed, and immunity to storms), asking:**  
“...how you hold the wave-floater steady, the sea-stallion, and steer it on the sound... yet I have never seen any man... steering at the stern.”[^3]

**Christ responds to Andrew's post-realization confession by reassuring him that he did not sin greatly and reminding him of past commands, before directing him to rescue Matthew.[^1]**

[^1]: Cynewulf, *Andreas*, chapter 1, [paragraph p-ee08ea](/works/cynewulf-andreas-07b573/01/translation/#p-ee08ea).
[^2]: Cynewulf, *Andreas*, chapter 1, [paragraph p-ee08ea](/works/cynewulf-andreas-07b573/01/translation/#p-ee08ea).
[^3]: Cynewulf, *Andreas*, chapter 1, [paragraph p-ca9772](/works/cynewulf-andreas-07b573/01/translation/#p-ca9772).
